Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You want to identify a specific person -- the one who leads the call. The best sentence is:
A "The partner who leads the call rarely says no." (defining, person = who)
B "The partner which leads the call rarely says no." (which is for things)
C "The partner, who leads the call, rarely says no." (commas make it non-essential, but here it is essential)
You want to add extra, non-essential information about silence. You say:
A "Silence that they find awkward is normal here." (no commas + that = defining)
B "Silence, which they find awkward, is normal here." (non-defining = commas + which)
C "Silence, that they find awkward, is normal here." (non-defining cannot use that)
You want to talk about the manager and his team's responsibility. Use:
A "The manager whose team handles procurement asked pointed questions." (possession = whose)
B "The manager who's team handles procurement asked pointed questions." (who's = who is)
C "The manager which team handles procurement asked pointed questions." (which cannot show possession of a person)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a cross-cultural negotiation, real or imagined. Talk about the people involved, the customs, the directness or silence, and how the team found common ground. Use at least four relative clauses.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1The negotiator who reads the room is the one who builds trust.O negociador que lê o ambiente é aquele que constrói confiança.
2In a high-context culture, you have to read between the lines.Em uma cultura de alto contexto, você precisa ler nas entrelinhas.
3The senior manager, whose approval we need, will speak last.O gerente sênior, cuja aprovação precisamos, falará por último.
4Directness, which can feel blunt, is a sign of respect in low-context cultures.Franqueza, que pode parecer direta demais, é sinal de respeito em culturas de baixo contexto.
5Let me build some common ground before we discuss the numbers.Deixe-me construir pontos em comum antes de discutirmos os números.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

The Risk Conversation -- Modals of Probability and Deduction

Modals of probability and deduction (must, can't, may, might, could; be likely / unlikely / bound to). New vocabulary: exposure, mitigate, threat, vulnerability, fallback, buffer, downside, likelihood, hedge, safeguard, off guard, downturn.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Exposure -- the degree to which a business is open to loss
"Our exposure to a single supplier is dangerously high." (Exposição ao risco)
Mitigate -- to reduce the seriousness or impact of a risk
"A two-week buffer helps mitigate shipping delays." (Mitigar / atenuar)
Threat -- a possible danger or source of harm to a plan
"Port congestion is the biggest threat to the launch." (Ameaça)
Vulnerability -- a weak point that could be exploited
"Relying on one factory is a serious vulnerability." (Vulnerabilidade)
Fallback -- an alternative plan used if the first one fails
"We have no fallback if this carrier goes bankrupt." (Plano alternativo)
Buffer -- extra time or stock that absorbs a delay or shock
"We build a two-week buffer into every shipment." (Margem de segurança)
Downside -- the negative side or potential loss of a decision
"The upside is speed; the downside is higher cost." (Lado negativo)
Likelihood -- how probable it is that something will happen
"The likelihood of a port delay is high this quarter." (Probabilidade)
Hedge -- an action taken to reduce financial loss
"Finance will hedge against the currency swing." (Proteção financeira / hedge)
Safeguard -- a measure put in place in advance to prevent harm
"A second supplier is our main safeguard." (Salvaguarda)
Off guard -- unprepared or surprised by an unexpected event
"The sudden price jump caught the team off guard." (Desprevenido)
Downturn -- a decline in business or economic activity
"A market downturn could cut demand by half." (Queda / retração)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Exposure
Mitigate
Threat
Vulnerability
Fallback
Buffer
Downside
Likelihood
Hedge
Safeguard
Off guard
Downturn

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

A good risk review names the uncertainty out loud. Port congestion might ease by Q3, but it could just as easily get worse, so we cannot assume smooth sailing. The exchange rate may move against us, and with thin margins, even a small swing could hurt. When every shipment arrives late, delays must be the main threat -- the evidence is too strong to ignore. A single-supplier model can't be safe; it is far too exposed. A default is unlikely to happen, but the impact would be severe, so it is worth a contingency. And doing nothing is bound to fail. The team that names the downside is the team that can mitigate it.

1. In "delays must be the main threat", what does "must" express?
A A logical deduction -- the speaker is almost certain it is true.
B An obligation -- the delays are required to happen.
C A possibility -- the speaker is unsure.
2. Why is "can't" used in "A single-supplier model can't be safe"?
A It expresses a lack of ability.
B It is a deduction -- the speaker is almost certain it is NOT true (impossible).
C It gives permission.
3. What is the difference between "might ease" and "is bound to fail"?
A "Might" is a possibility; "bound to" is near-certain.
B They mean exactly the same thing.
C "Might" is past; "bound to" is future.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modals of Probability

GRAMMAR

Modais para expressar o quanto você tem certeza sobre o presente e o futuro. / Modals to express how sure you are about the present and the future.

ModalCerteza / CertaintyExample / Exemplo
mustQuase certo que É verdade (dedução) / Almost certain (true)Delays must be the main threat.
can'tQuase certo que NÃO é (dedução) / Almost certain (false)That can't be our only option.
may / might / couldPossibilidade real / A real possibilityThe rate may move against us.
be likely / bound toProvável / quase certo que ocorre / Probable, near-certainDelays are likely to continue.
Ponto-chaveDepois do modal, use o verbo na FORMA BASE -- sem to e sem -s (might GET, could HURT). / After a modal, use the base verb.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Every shipment is late, so delays be the main threat."
"The exchange rate move against us this quarter."
"This be our only supplier; it is far too risky."
"A default is happen, but the impact would be severe."
"With no hedge, we are be caught off guard."
"A two-week buffer helps us shipping delays."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?He weighs the likelihood and the impact of each one.
?Marcus opens the risk review before the team signs off.
?The team agrees that a risk you can name is a risk you can manage.
?He recommends a buffer, a second supplier, and a hedge.
?He flags three threats: port congestion, supplier exposure, and currency swings.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

A risk you can name is a risk you can mitigate.
Um risco que você consegue nomear é um risco que você consegue mitigar.
It might ease by Q3, but it could just as easily get worse.
Pode melhorar até o terceiro trimestre, mas poderia facilmente piorar.
A default is unlikely, but the impact would be severe.
Uma inadimplência é improvável, mas o impacto seria grave.
Let me build in a buffer so we are not caught off guard.
Deixe-me incluir uma margem de segurança para não sermos pegos desprevenidos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

The evidence is overwhelming and you want to state a near-certain deduction. You say:
A "Delays must be the main threat." (deduction = must)
B "Delays must to be the main threat." (no to after a modal)
C "Delays have to be the main threat." (have to = obligation, not deduction)
You want to flag a possibility you are not sure about. The best sentence is:
A "The rate will move against us." (will = certainty, too strong)
B "The rate might move against us." (might = possibility)
C "The rate might moves against us." (no -s after a modal)
You are almost certain a single-supplier model is NOT safe. You say:
A "This can't be safe -- it is far too exposed." (deduction = can't)
B "This mustn't be safe -- it is far too exposed." (mustn't = prohibition, not deduction)
C "This couldn't to be safe." (no to after a modal)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a real risk in your work, real or imagined. Talk about the threat, how likely it is, the downside if it happens, and how you would mitigate it. Use at least four modals of probability.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1A risk you can name is a risk you can mitigate.Um risco que você consegue nomear é um risco que você consegue mitigar.
2It might ease by Q3, but it could just as easily get worse.Pode melhorar até o terceiro trimestre, mas poderia facilmente piorar.
3A default is unlikely, but the impact would be severe.Uma inadimplência é improvável, mas o impacto seria grave.
4Let me build in a buffer so we are not caught off guard.Deixe-me incluir uma margem de segurança para não sermos pegos desprevenidos.
5Doing nothing is the one option that is bound to fail.Não fazer nada é a única opção fadada ao fracasso.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

The Decision Meeting -- Reported Speech, Recommending and Reaching Consensus

Reported speech (statements with backshift, reported questions with if/whether, recommend/suggest/propose that + base verb). New vocabulary: consensus, endorse, defer, weigh up, ratify, veto, shortlist, finalize, deadlock, abstain, recommendation, unanimous.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Consensus -- general agreement reached by a whole group
"We are close to consensus on the dual-supplier plan." (Consenso)
Endorse -- to give formal support or approval to a plan
"Which sourcing option do we endorse today?" (Endossar / apoiar)
Defer -- to delay a decision until a later time
"Let us not defer this decision any further." (Adiar)
Weigh up -- to carefully consider and compare two options
"Weigh up the cost against the protection." (Ponderar / avaliar)
Ratify -- to make a decision official by formal approval
"We recommend that the board ratify the top two vendors." (Ratificar / homologar)
Veto -- to formally reject or block a proposal
"Nobody chose to veto the plan." (Vetar)
Shortlist -- a reduced list of the best candidates or options
"Operations drew up a shortlist of three vendors." (Lista final / pré-selecionados)
Finalize -- to complete and confirm something as final
"I will finalize the paperwork by Friday." (Finalizar / concluir)
Deadlock -- a situation where no agreement can be reached
"Let us not leave this room in deadlock." (Impasse)
Abstain -- to choose not to vote on a decision
"One colleague decided to abstain." (Abster-se)
Recommendation -- a formal suggestion about what should be done
"My recommendation is to ratify the top two vendors." (Recomendação)
Unanimous -- supported by everyone, with no one against
"The vote was almost unanimous, with one abstention." (Unânime)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Consensus
Endorse
Defer
Weigh up
Ratify
Veto
Shortlist
Finalize
Deadlock
Abstain
Recommendation
Unanimous

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

A good chair relays the room before the vote. Marcus reminded everyone that the risk review had flagged the single-supplier exposure. Finance said the dual-supplier plan would cost more, but they advised that it would protect us against a default. Operations told the room they had drawn up a shortlist, and they recommended that the board ratify the top two vendors. Legal asked whether the contracts could be signed before the end of the month. Notice how each reported sentence shifts the tense back and drops the question mark. Nobody chose to veto, one colleague said she would abstain, and the rest signed off, so the room reached consensus.

1. The original was "The plan will cost more." Why does the report say "would cost"?
A After a past reporting verb, "will" shifts back to "would".
B "Would" expresses a polite request.
C "Would" and "will" are interchangeable.
2. Why is "whether" used in "Legal asked whether the contracts could be signed"?
A It introduces a wh- question.
B It reports a yes/no question, which uses if or whether.
C It shows the speaker is uncertain about the grammar.
3. Why is the base verb "ratify" used after "recommended that"?
A Recommend / suggest / propose that take the base verb for every subject.
B It is a typing error for "ratifies".
C The subject "board" is plural.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

Como relatar o que alguém disse, perguntou ou recomendou. / How to report what someone said, asked, or recommended.

Tipo / TypeMudança / ShiftExample / Exemplo
Statementpresente → passado; will → would; can → couldShe said the plan cost more.
Perfectpresent perfect → past perfect (have → had)They said they had drawn up a list.
Wh- questionordem de afirmação, sem do/does/did / statement orderHe asked which option we would endorse.
Yes/no questionuse if / whetherShe asked whether we could sign.
Ponto-chaveApós recommend / suggest / propose that, use o verbo na FORMA BASE para qualquer sujeito (that we ratify). / Use the base verb after recommend that.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each reported sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Direct "The plan costs more." → "Finance said the plan more than the alternative."
Direct "It will protect us." → "They advised that it protect us from a default."
Direct "Which vendor do we endorse?" → "Marcus asked which vendor we ."
Direct "Can we sign now?" → "Legal asked we could sign now."
Direct "Ratify the top two." → "They recommended that we the top two vendors."
Direct "We have reached consensus." → "She said they reached consensus on the plan."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?Operations presents a shortlist and recommends ratifying the top two.
?Marcus opens the meeting and reminds the room of the supplier risk.
?Everyone signs off except one colleague, who abstains, and the motion carries.
?The group weighs up cost against protection and reaches consensus.
?Finance reports the plan costs more but protects against a default.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

They told me the plan would cost more, but advised that it would protect us.
Eles me disseram que o plano custaria mais, mas aconselharam que ele nos protegeria.
Marcus asked which option we would endorse.
Marcus perguntou qual opção nós endossaríamos.
They recommended that we ratify the top two vendors.
Eles recomendaram que ratificássemos os dois principais fornecedores.
We are close to consensus, so let us not leave the room in deadlock.
Estamos perto do consenso, então não deixemos a sala em impasse.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You are relaying a wh- question your manager asked earlier. You say:
A "She asked which vendor we would choose." (statement order)
B "She asked which vendor do we choose." (no do in a reported question)
C "She asked which vendor we will choose?" (no question mark; backshift will to would)
You want to report a recommendation correctly. The best sentence is:
A "They recommended that we ratifies it." (base verb, not ratifies)
B "They recommended that we ratify it." (recommend that + base verb)
C "They recommended that we will ratify it." (no will after recommend that)
You are reporting a yes/no question Legal asked. You say:
A "Legal asked whether the contracts could be signed." (if/whether + backshift)
B "Legal asked can the contracts be signed." (no question word order in reports)
C "Legal asked that the contracts could be signed." (use whether, not that, for a question)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Report a real decision meeting from your work, real or imagined. Say who recommended what, what questions came up, and how the group reached consensus. Use at least four reported-speech structures.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1They told me the plan would cost more, but advised that it would protect us.Eles me disseram que o plano custaria mais, mas aconselharam que ele nos protegeria.
2Marcus asked which option we would endorse.Marcus perguntou qual opção nós endossaríamos.
3They recommended that we ratify the top two vendors.Eles recomendaram que ratificássemos os dois principais fornecedores.
4We are close to consensus, so let us not leave the room in deadlock.Estamos perto do consenso, então não deixemos a sala em impasse.
5A decision deferred is a risk renewed.Uma decisão adiada é um risco renovado.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Negotiating the Contract -- Diplomatic Language: Softening and Hedging

Diplomatic language (modal softeners, qualifiers, understatement, "it might be worth + -ing", indirect questions). New vocabulary: diplomatic, goodwill, amenable, constructive, sticking point, wiggle room, caveat, reservation, hammer out, defuse, understate, tentative.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Diplomatic -- careful and tactful when dealing with people
"Keep the language diplomatic when you push back on price." (Diplomático)
Goodwill -- a friendly, cooperative attitude between two sides
"We protect the goodwill so both sides will sign." (Boa vontade / relacionamento)
Amenable -- willing to agree, cooperate, or be persuaded
"A supplier who feels respected is amenable." (Receptivo / aberto)
Constructive -- helpful and intended to improve a situation
"Instead of rejecting it, we offered a constructive alternative." (Construtivo)
Sticking point -- an issue that blocks agreement from being reached
"The penalty clause was the only sticking point left." (Ponto de conflito)
Wiggle room -- room or flexibility to change or negotiate
"Is there any wiggle room on the delivery schedule?" (Margem de negociação)
Caveat -- a warning or condition attached to a statement
"I agree, with one caveat: confirm it in writing by Friday." (Ressalva)
Reservation -- a concern or doubt about a proposal
"We have one or two reservations about the penalty clause." (Reserva / ressalva)
Hammer out -- to reach an agreement after long, hard discussion
"Let us hammer out the final terms before Friday." (Negociar até chegar a um acordo)
Defuse -- to reduce tension in a difficult situation
"If the room gets tense, defuse it with a question." (Amenizar / desarmar)
Understate -- to present something as smaller or less serious
"Understate the problem: say it is a slight concern." (Minimizar / suavizar)
Tentative -- not firm or final; provisional and open to change
"We reached a tentative agreement on price." (Provisório / preliminar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Diplomatic
Goodwill
Amenable
Constructive
Sticking point
Wiggle room
Caveat
Reservation
Hammer out
Defuse
Understate
Tentative

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

A skilled negotiator never sounds like an order. Instead of "Lower the price," she says the price might be a little high and asks whether there is any wiggle room. Instead of "This clause is wrong," she says she has one or two reservations about it. When a point becomes a sticking point, she does not reject it; she suggests it might be worth revisiting the wording. To ask for a favour, she uses an indirect question: "I was wondering whether you could be flexible on the deadline." Notice that each move adds a modal, a qualifier, or an indirect frame to soften the message and protect the goodwill, so the other side stays amenable.

1. Why does the negotiator say "the price might be a little high" instead of "the price is too high"?
A The modal "might" plus the qualifier "a little" soften the criticism.
B "Might" means she is unsure of the actual price.
C The two sentences mean exactly the same thing.
2. Why use "I was wondering whether you could..." instead of "Can you...?"
A It is grammatically required after a comma.
B The indirect frame makes the request more polite and less pushy.
C It is only used in writing, never in speech.
3. What does "it might be worth revisiting the wording" do?
A It turns a demand into a soft suggestion ("it might be worth + -ing").
B It states that the wording is definitely worthless.
C It is an order to rewrite the clause immediately.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Diplomatic Language

GRAMMAR

Como suavizar uma crítica, um pedido ou uma discordância para soar diplomático. / How to soften a criticism, a request, or a disagreement to sound diplomatic.

Recurso / MoveComo / HowExample / Exemplo
Modal softenersuse might / could / would em vez de is / mustThe price might be high.
Qualifiersa little / a bit / slightly / one or two (suavizadores)We have one or two reservations.
Understatementdizer menos do que se quer (not quite / not sure)I am not sure I fully agree.
SuggestingIt might be worth + -ing; I'd suggest + -ingIt might be worth revisiting it.
Ponto-chavePara um pedido educado, use uma pergunta indireta: I was wondering whether... ou Would it be possible to...? em vez do imperativo. / Use an indirect question for a polite request.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each diplomatic sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Blunt "The price is too high." → "The price be a little high for our budget."
Blunt "Give us a discount." → "I was whether there might be a discount."
Blunt "Change the clause." → "It might be revisiting the clause."
Blunt "Lower the price." → "Would it be to lower the price a little?"
Blunt "We refuse this term." → "We have one or two about this term."
Blunt "I disagree." → "I am not I fully agree with that."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?The supplier raises a sticking point on the penalty clause.
?Marcus sets the tone and asks the team to keep the language diplomatic.
?Both sides protect the goodwill and hammer out the final terms.
?Fabiana softens the demand and offers a constructive alternative with one caveat.
?Fabiana opens by understating the problem and asking about wiggle room on price.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

The price might be a little high. Is there any wiggle room?
O preço pode estar um pouco alto. Há alguma margem de negociação?
We have one or two reservations about this clause.
Temos uma ou duas reservas em relação a esta cláusula.
It might be worth revisiting the notice period.
Talvez valha a pena rever o prazo de aviso prévio.
I was wondering whether there might be any flexibility on price.
Eu queria saber se haveria alguma flexibilidade no preço.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You want to push back on price without offending the supplier. You say:
A "The price might be a little high. Is there any wiggle room?" (modal + qualifier)
B "Your price is too high. Lower it." (blunt imperative damages goodwill)
C "The price is unacceptable and final." (no room to negotiate)
A clause is a problem, but you want to keep it constructive. The best line is:
A "This clause is completely wrong." (blunt judgement)
B "We have one or two reservations -- it might be worth revisiting it." (soft noun + suggestion)
C "Delete the clause now." (an order, not a negotiation)
You want to make a polite request for flexibility. You say:
A "I was wondering whether you could be flexible on the deadline." (indirect question)
B "Be flexible on the deadline." (a command)
C "You must change the deadline." (no goodwill)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a real negotiation from your work, real or imagined. Push back on one point and concede on another, using diplomatic language. Use at least four softening structures (modal softeners, qualifiers, understatement, or an indirect question).

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1The price might be a little high. Is there any wiggle room?O preço pode estar um pouco alto. Há alguma margem de negociação?
2We have one or two reservations about this clause.Temos uma ou duas reservas em relação a esta cláusula.
3It might be worth revisiting the notice period.Talvez valha a pena rever o prazo de aviso prévio.
4I can live with that, with one caveat: confirm it in writing.Posso aceitar isso, com uma ressalva: confirme por escrito.
5Protect the goodwill, and the deal protects itself.Proteja a boa vontade, e o acordo se protege sozinho.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Managing Conflict at Work -- Concession & Contrast Clauses

Concession and contrast (although, even though, while, whereas, despite / in spite of, however) for handling disagreement and giving difficult feedback. New vocabulary: friction, grievance, defensive, de-escalate, accountability, candid, empathy, blame, pushback, overstep, assertive, standoff.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Friction -- ongoing tension or low-level conflict between people
"There is clearly some friction between the two teams." (Atrito)
Grievance -- a formal complaint about unfair treatment
"A couple of people raised a grievance about the delay." (Queixa formal)
Defensive -- reacting to criticism by protecting yourself instead of listening
"He got defensive the moment I raised the deadline." (Na defensiva)
De-escalate -- to reduce the intensity of a conflict or tense situation
"Our job today is to de-escalate, not to win." (Reduzir a tensão)
Accountability -- owning the result of your actions and decisions
"We want accountability for the process, not blame." (Responsabilização)
Candid -- honest and direct, even about hard things, but still kind
"I had to be candid about the impact of the slip." (Franco / sincero)
Empathy -- the ability to understand and share how another person feels
"Lead with empathy before you make your point." (Empatia)
Blame -- saying that someone is responsible for something bad
"Focus on the process, not on blame for the people." (Culpa)
Pushback -- firm resistance or opposition to an idea or decision
"I expected pushback on the new tracking standard." (Resistência)
Overstep -- to go beyond the limits of your role or authority
"I did not want to overstep, so I asked first." (Extrapolar / passar dos limites)
Assertive -- speaking up confidently and firmly, without being aggressive
"Be assertive about the standard, but never aggressive." (Assertivo)
Standoff -- a deadlock where neither side will give ground
"Last time the conversation ended in a standoff." (Impasse)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Friction
Grievance
Defensive
De-escalate
Accountability
Candid
Empathy
Blame
Pushback
Overstep
Assertive
Standoff

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Giving difficult feedback is really about acknowledging one thing and countering another in the same breath. A skilled manager never just blames. She says, "Although the deadline slipped, I know you were working with a broken forecast." She concedes the pressure -- "While I understand the workload, the slip still hurt the client" -- and then contrasts two facts: "The cost stayed flat, whereas the lead time doubled." When she wants to keep the goodwill, she leads with the positive: "Despite the friction, your team responded well." Notice that although, even though, while and whereas are followed by a full clause, but despite and in spite of are followed by a noun. Used well, these linkers de-escalate the conflict and turn blame into shared accountability.

1. Why does the manager say "Although the deadline slipped, I know you had a broken forecast"?
A She acknowledges the problem first, then concedes a reason -- it softens the feedback.
B "Although" means she is not sure the deadline really slipped.
C The two clauses contradict each other and cancel out.
2. What is the difference between "despite" and "although" in the text?
A They are interchangeable in every position.
B "Despite" is followed by a noun, while "although" is followed by a full clause.
C "Despite" can only be used at the end of a sentence.
3. What does "whereas" do in "The cost stayed flat, whereas the lead time doubled"?
A It directly contrasts two facts inside one sentence.
B It shows that the two facts caused each other.
C It means "because" -- it gives the reason.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Concession & Contrast

GRAMMAR

Como reconhecer um ponto e contra-argumentar na mesma frase para suavizar uma crítica. / How to acknowledge a point and counter it in the same sentence to soften feedback.

Conector / LinkerO que vem depois / What followsExample / Exemplo
Although / Even thoughuma frase completa (sujeito + verbo) / a full clauseAlthough the team missed the deadline...
Whileuma frase; concede um ponto / a clause, concedes a pointWhile I understand the pressure...
Whereasuma frase; contraste direto de dois fatos / a clause, direct contrastCost stayed flat, whereas lead time doubled.
Despite / In spite ofum SUBSTANTIVO ou -ing (nunca uma frase) / a NOUN or -ingDespite the friction, we agreed.
Ponto-chaveCuidado: Despite the delay (substantivo) e correto, mas Despite the team was late é ERRADO -- use Although the team was late. / Despite + noun, not a clause.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the right linker or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Acknowledge + counter: " the deadline slipped, I know the forecast was broken."
Noun follows: " the friction, we found common ground."
Direct contrast: "The cost was on target, the lead time doubled."
Concede a point: " I understand the pressure, I have to be candid about the impact."
Aim of the meeting: "Our job is to the friction, not to win."
Goal of feedback: "We want for the process, not blame for the people."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?The logistics lead gets defensive when the missed deadline is raised.
?Marcus briefs the team and asks them to de-escalate the friction.
?Both sides reach common ground and agree on shared accountability.
?Fabiana stays candid about the impact but keeps the focus on the process.
?Fabiana opens by leading with empathy and acknowledging the broken forecast.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Although the deadline slipped, I know the forecast was broken.
Embora o prazo tenha estourado, sei que a previsão estava furada.
Despite the friction, I think we can find common ground.
Apesar do atrito, acho que podemos encontrar um terreno comum.
The accountability sits with the process, not the people.
A responsabilização é do processo, não das pessoas.
While I understand the pressure, the slip still hurt the client.
Embora eu entenda a pressão, o atraso ainda prejudicou o cliente.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

A teammate missed a deadline, but the forecast was broken. You want to give feedback without blame. You say:
A "Although the deadline slipped, I know you were working with a broken forecast." (acknowledge + counter)
B "You missed the deadline. This is on you." (pure blame -- triggers defensiveness)
C "It does not matter, forget it." (avoids the feedback entirely)
There was tension, but the team still agreed. You want to use "despite" correctly. The best line is:
A "Despite there was friction, we agreed." (despite cannot take a clause)
B "Despite the friction, we agreed." (despite + noun)
C "Despite of the friction, we agreed." ("despite of" is not correct English)
The colleague gets defensive and feels blamed. You want to de-escalate. You say:
A "I am not here to blame anyone -- the accountability sits with the process." (redirects from people to process)
B "You are being too defensive about this." (escalates the conflict)
C "Fine, it is all your fault then." (sarcasm, pure blame)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a real disagreement or piece of difficult feedback from your work, real or imagined. Acknowledge the other side, then make your point, using at least four concession or contrast structures (although, even though, while, whereas, despite).

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Although the deadline slipped, I know the forecast was broken.Embora o prazo tenha estourado, sei que a previsão estava furada.
2While I understand the pressure, the slip did hurt the client.Embora eu entenda a pressão, o atraso prejudicou o cliente.
3Despite the friction, I think we can find common ground.Apesar do atrito, acho que podemos encontrar um terreno comum.
4The accountability sits with the process, not the people.A responsabilização é do processo, não das pessoas.
5Lead with empathy, and the hard feedback lands.Comece com empatia, e o feedback difícil é bem recebido.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Leading a Project Kick-off -- Future Continuous (will be + -ing)

Future continuous (will be + verb-ing) for describing actions in progress across a project timeline, contrasted with future perfect (will have + past participle). New vocabulary: kick-off, deliverable, work stream, dependency, onboard, ramp up, cadence, scope creep, charter, stakeholder map, ownership, success criteria.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Kick-off -- the first meeting that officially launches a project
"Welcome to the kick-off for the supplier-integration project." (Reunião de abertura)
Deliverable -- a concrete result the project must produce and hand over
"The deliverables for phase one are the charter and the map." (Entregável)
Work stream -- a self-contained part of the project with its own owner and goal
"Each work stream has a single owner."
Dependency -- when one task or stream relies on another before it can start
"The data stream depends on contracts, so watch that dependency." (Dependência)
Onboard -- to bring a new supplier, partner, or member into the system
"We will be onboarding four new suppliers." (Integrar)
Ramp up -- to increase activity or output gradually, step by step
"We will be ramping up the pilot gradually, not all at once." (Acelerar gradualmente)
Cadence -- the regular, predictable rhythm of meetings or check-ins
"I will be running the kick-off cadence personally." (Ritmo / cafência)
Scope creep -- uncontrolled growth in what a project is expected to cover
"Guard against scope creep: new requests go through me." (Expansão descontrolada do escopo)
Charter -- the founding document that defines a project's goal, scope, and owners
"The project charter and the stakeholder map are due by week two."
Stakeholder map -- a view of everyone affected by the project and how much each one cares
"The stakeholder map shows who we will be keeping informed."
Ownership -- being the single person clearly responsible for a result
"Ownership is clear from day one: one owner per stream." (Titularidade / responsabilidade clara)
Success criteria -- the clear, agreed measures that define whether a project succeeded
"The success criteria are on-time onboarding, clean data, zero disruption." (Critérios de sucesso)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Kick-off
Deliverable
Work stream
Dependency
Onboard
Ramp up
Cadence
Scope creep
Charter
Stakeholder map
Ownership
Success criteria

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

A strong kick-off is mostly about painting the timeline. Instead of vague promises, a good leader describes what the team will be doing at each point. "Over the next three months, we will be onboarding four suppliers," she says, "and I will be running the cadence for the first weeks." The future continuous -- will be + verb-ing -- shows an action that is in progress at a future moment, not finished. She uses it for the whole rollout: "While we ramp up, I will be tracking the success criteria." For things that are complete by a deadline, she switches to the future perfect: "By the end of week two, we will have delivered the charter." Notice the difference: will be delivering means still in progress, while will have delivered means already done.

1. Why does the leader say "we will be onboarding four suppliers" instead of "we will onboard four suppliers"?
A The future continuous shows the action in progress across the whole period, not as a single event.
B "Will be onboarding" means she is not sure it will happen.
C The two forms mean exactly the same thing in every case.
2. What is the difference between "will be delivering" and "will have delivered"?
A They are interchangeable.
B "Will be delivering" is still in progress; "will have delivered" is already finished by that point.
C "Will have delivered" is a question form.
3. How is the future continuous formed?
A will + be + verb-ing (e.g., will be tracking).
B will + have + past participle.
C will + base verb only.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Future Continuous

GRAMMAR

Como descrever uma ação em andamento num momento futuro -- a base de um bom kick-off. / How to describe an action in progress at a future moment -- the backbone of a good kick-off.

Forma / FormEstrutura / StructureExample / Exemplo
Afirmativa / Affirmativewill + be + verbo-ingWe will be onboarding four suppliers.
Negativa / Negativewill not (won't) + be + verbo-ingWe won't be ramping up all at once.
Pergunta / QuestionWill + sujeito + be + verbo-ing?Will you be running the cadence?
Significado / Meaningação EM ANDAMENTO num momento futuro / action in progress at a future momentAt 3 pm we will be testing the pilot.
Ponto-chaveCompare: will be delivering (ainda em andamento) vs will have delivered (já terminado até aquele momento). / In progress vs finished by then.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the right form or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

In progress across a period: "Over the next month, we four suppliers."
At a future moment: "This time next week, I the cadence call."
Finished by a deadline: "By the end of week two, we the charter."
Part of the project: "Each has a single owner, so ownership is clear."
Risk to control: "Guard against , so new requests go through me first."
Relies on another: "The data stream cannot start until contracts close -- that is the ."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?He flags the dependency between the contracts stream and the data stream.
?Marcus opens the kick-off and sets the direction for the project.
?Fabiana confirms the charter and the map are signed off and parks two extra requests.
?The team agrees to ramp up the pilot gradually while tracking the success criteria.
?He assigns a single owner to each work stream so ownership is clear.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Over the next three months, we will be onboarding four suppliers.
Nos próximos três meses, estaremos integrando quatro fornecedores.
Each work stream has a single owner, so ownership is clear.
Cada frente de trabalho tem um único responsável, então a titularidade fica clara.
By week two, we will have delivered the charter and the map.
Até a segunda semana, teremos entregue o documento fundador e o mapa.
Guard against scope creep: new requests come to me first.
Cuidado com a expansão do escopo: pedidos novos passam por mim primeiro.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You are opening a kick-off and want to describe what the team will be doing across the next quarter. You say:
A "Over the next quarter, we will be onboarding the new suppliers one at a time." (future continuous, in progress)
B "We will onboarded the suppliers." (wrong form)
C "We are going to do many things." (vague, no timeline)
A deliverable must be finished by a specific deadline. The best line is:
A "By week two, we will be delivering the charter." (still in progress -- wrong for a deadline)
B "By week two, we will have delivered the charter." (future perfect -- finished by then)
C "By week two, we will deliver maybe the charter." (vague and ungrammatical)
A new request lands mid-project and could expand the scope. As the leader, you say:
A "To avoid scope creep, that request comes to me first before it enters a work stream." (protects the scope)
B "Sure, add it straight into the data stream." (invites scope creep)
C "Ignore the request completely." (avoids the stakeholder)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Open the kick-off for a real or imagined project from your work. Set the direction, assign ownership of the work streams, flag one dependency, and describe what the team will be doing across the timeline, using at least four future-continuous structures (will be + verb-ing).

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1Over the next three months, we will be onboarding four suppliers.Nos próximos três meses, estaremos integrando quatro fornecedores.
2Each work stream has a single owner, so ownership is clear.Cada frente de trabalho tem um único responsável, então a titularidade fica clara.
3Watch the dependency: data cannot start until contracts close.Cuidado com a dependência: dados não começam até contratos fecharem.
4By week two, we will have delivered the charter and the map.Até a segunda semana, teremos entregue o documento fundador e o mapa.
5Guard against scope creep: new requests come to me first.Cuidado com a expansão do escopo: pedidos novos passam por mim primeiro.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Disruption Management -- The Subjunctive (It is essential that + base verb)

The subjunctive in formal recommendations (It is essential/critical/imperative that + subject + base verb, e.g., "that the team be notified", "that we escalate", "that the supplier provide") for communicating a crisis to leadership with urgency. New vocabulary: outage, breach, containment, flag up, recovery plan, postmortem, stopgap, severity, notify, brief, fallout, damage control.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Outage -- a period when a system or service stops working
"At two a.m. we had a system outage at the main hub." (Queda / interrupção do sistema)
Breach -- a serious failure or break of security, a contract, or a protocol
"The outage was followed by a data breach in the portal." (Violação)
Containment -- the act of stopping a problem from spreading further
"Containment is in progress, so the failure will not spread." (Contenção)
Flag up -- to raise an issue urgently so a higher level sees it
"I recommend that we flag up any further failure to me directly." (Sinalizar / alertar)
Recovery plan -- a documented set of steps to restore normal operations
"I asked that each supplier provide a written recovery plan." (Plano de recuperação)
Postmortem -- a review held after an incident to learn what went wrong
"We will run a postmortem to find the root issue." (Análise pós-incidente)
Stopgap -- a temporary fix used until a permanent solution is ready
"The team applied a stopgap that keeps orders flowing." (Solução paliativa)
Severity -- how serious or damaging an incident is
"The severity is high, so we treat it as priority one." (Gravidade)
Notify -- to formally inform someone of something
"It is critical that the suppliers be notified within the hour." (Notificar)
Brief -- to give someone the key facts they need, concisely (verb)
"It is essential that leadership be briefed before the market opens." (Informar / dar um briefing)
Fallout -- the negative consequences that follow an incident
"On the fallout side, we should prepare a statement now." (Repercussões / consequências)
Damage control -- actions taken to limit harm to reputation or operations
"For damage control, no one should comment publicly yet." (Controle de danos)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Outage
Breach
Containment
Flag up
Recovery plan
Postmortem
Stopgap
Severity
Notify
Brief
Fallout
Damage control

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

When you communicate a crisis to leadership, the language has to sound formal and urgent. English does this with the subjunctive. After triggers like it is essential / critical / imperative, or verbs like recommend, insist, and ask, we add that + a subject + the base form of the verb. Two things change. First, the verb takes no -s, even for he, she, or it: "We ask that each supplier provide a recovery plan" -- not "provides". Second, the verb "be" stays as be, never is or are: "It is essential that leadership be briefed", "It is critical that the team be notified". So in a briefing you might say: "The severity is high. I recommend that we escalate immediately, and it is imperative that no one comment publicly until our statement is ready."

1. After "It is essential that leadership ___ briefed", which word is correct?
A be -- the subjunctive keeps the verb "be" as the base form.
B is -- because leadership is singular.
C are -- because it sounds more formal.
2. Why do we say "We ask that each supplier provide" and not "provides"?
A Because "supplier" is plural.
B The subjunctive uses the base form, so there is no -s even for he/she/it.
C "Provides" is simply a spelling mistake.
3. Which structure triggers the subjunctive?
A It is essential / I recommend / we insist + that + subject + base verb.
B will + be + verb-ing.
C have + past participle.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Subjunctive

GRAMMAR

Como fazer recomendações formais e urgentes -- a base de um bom briefing de crise. / How to make formal, urgent recommendations -- the backbone of a good crisis briefing.

Gatilho / TriggerEstrutura / StructureExample / Exemplo
It is essential / critical / imperative+ that + sujeito + verbo na forma baseIt is essential that the team be notified.
recommend / suggest / propose+ that + sujeito + verbo baseI recommend that we escalate now.
ask / request / insist+ that + sujeito + verbo baseWe ask that the supplier provide a plan.
Regra 1 / Key 1: sem -she/she/it mantém o verbo base / no -sthat he provide (não provides)
Regra 2 / Key 2: be, não is/areO verbo "be" fica como be: that leadership be briefed (não is/are). / The verb "be" stays as be.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the right form or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Subjunctive "be": "It is essential that leadership briefed before the market opens."
Recommend + base verb: "I recommend that we immediately."
No -s for the supplier: "We ask that each supplier a recovery plan."
How serious it is: "The is high, so we treat this as priority one."
Temporary fix: "The team applied a that keeps orders flowing."
Limit harm to reputation: "For , no one should comment publicly yet."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?Marcus insists that leadership be briefed and the suppliers be notified within the hour.
?Overnight, a system outage at the hub triggers a data breach in the supplier portal.
?Fabiana asks that each supplier provide a recovery plan and proposes a postmortem.
?The team applies a stopgap so orders keep flowing while containment is in progress.
?Marcus rates the severity as high and recommends treating it as priority one.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

It is essential that leadership be briefed before the market opens.
É essencial que a liderança seja informada antes de o mercado abrir.
I recommend that we escalate immediately and treat this as priority one.
Recomendo que escalemos imediatamente e tratemos isso como prioridade um.
We ask that each supplier provide a recovery plan by end of day.
Pedimos que cada fornecedor forneça um plano de recuperação até o fim do dia.
For damage control, I propose that no one comment publicly yet.
Para controle de danos, proponho que ninguém se manifeste publicamente ainda.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

An outage just hit and leadership has not been told. You want to recommend, formally and urgently, that they be informed. You say:
A "It is essential that leadership be informed now, and I recommend that we escalate immediately." (subjunctive, formal)
B "It is essential that leadership is informed." (wrong: should be "be")
C "Maybe someone could tell them at some point." (vague, not urgent)
You want each affected supplier to send a recovery plan. The best, most formal line is:
A "We ask that the supplier provides a recovery plan." (wrong: no -s in the subjunctive)
B "We ask that each supplier provide a recovery plan by end of day." (base form, correct)
C "Tell the supplier to maybe make a plan." (informal, vague)
News could leak before your statement is ready. For damage control, you recommend:
A "It is imperative that no one comment publicly until the statement is ready." (protects the narrative)
B "Everyone should post about it right away." (worsens the fallout)
C "It is imperative that no one comments." (wrong: no -s -- should be "comment")

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Brief leadership on a real or imagined disruption from your work. Describe the incident, rate its severity, and make at least four formal recommendations using the subjunctive (It is essential that ... be ..., I recommend that we ..., we ask that the supplier provide ...).

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1It is essential that leadership be briefed before the market opens.É essencial que a liderança seja informada antes de o mercado abrir.
2I recommend that we escalate immediately and treat this as priority one.Recomendo que escalemos imediatamente e tratemos isso como prioridade um.
3It is critical that the affected suppliers be notified within the hour.É crítico que os fornecedores afetados sejam notificados dentro de uma hora.
4We ask that each supplier provide a recovery plan by end of day.Pedimos que cada fornecedor forneça um plano de recuperação até o fim do dia.
5For damage control, I propose that no one comment publicly yet.Para controle de danos, proponho que ninguém se manifeste publicamente ainda.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Strategic Proposals -- Complex Noun Phrases (a phased rollout designed to minimize disruption)

Building dense, precise noun phrases that pack information before and after the head noun (pre-modifiers + post-modifying of/from/designed-to phrases, e.g., "a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal", "the projected savings from the renegotiated freight contracts") -- the language of authoritative written and spoken proposals. New vocabulary: rationale, consolidation, payback period, return on investment, staged approach, cost-benefit, value driver, underpin, compelling, headline figure, quantify, business case.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Rationale -- the reasons that justify a decision or proposal
"Let me give you the rationale for moving from eleven suppliers to four." (Justificativa / racional)
Consolidation -- combining several things into one to gain efficiency
"The supplier consolidation reduces eleven contracts to four." (Consolidação)
Payback period -- the time needed to recover an investment
"The payback period is under eleven months." (Período de retorno / payback)
Return on investment -- the gain produced relative to the cost
"That gives us a return on investment of nearly two hundred and eighty percent." (Retorno sobre o investimento)
Staged approach -- a plan carried out in deliberate, separate steps
"I propose a staged approach, region by region." (Abordagem em etapas / faseada)
Cost-benefit -- weighing the gains against the costs
"The cost-benefit is clear: low upfront cost, fast payback." (Custo-benefício)
Value driver -- a factor that most increases value or savings
"The single biggest value driver is volume." (Fator de valor / alavanca de valor)
Underpin -- to support and strengthen the basis of something
"Real figures underpin every claim in the proposal." (Sustentar / embasar)
Compelling -- convincing and persuasive enough to act on
"This is a compelling, low-risk path to a leaner supply base." (Convincente)
Headline figure -- the single most important number in a proposal
"Let me start with the headline figure: four point two million in savings." (Número principal / cifra de destaque)
Quantify -- to express something as a precise number
"I have tried to quantify every assumption in the model." (Quantificar)
Business case -- the documented justification for an investment or project
"The business case is built on real figures, not estimates." (Caso de negócio / justificativa)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Rationale
Consolidation
Payback period
Return on investment
Staged approach
Cost-benefit
Value driver
Underpin
Compelling
Headline figure
Quantify
Business case

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The language of a strong proposal is dense. Instead of writing a long sentence, English packs information into one complex noun phrase -- a head noun loaded on both sides. Before the head noun we add pre-modifiers: "a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal". Here proposal is the head noun, and fully costed, three-year, and supplier-consolidation all stack in front of it. After the head noun we add post-modifiers -- usually a prepositional phrase (of, from, in) or a participle phrase (designed to, based on): "the projected savings from the renegotiated freight contracts", "a phased rollout designed to minimize disruption". The head noun is savings and rollout; everything after it modifies it. The result sounds authoritative and precise -- exactly what leadership wants in a business case. So instead of "We have a plan and it will save money and it will take three years", you say: "a three-year consolidation plan with projected savings of four million dollars".

1. In "a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal", what is the head noun?
A proposal -- the words before it are all pre-modifiers describing it.
B costed -- because it comes first.
C three-year -- because it gives the time.
2. In "the projected savings from the renegotiated freight contracts", which part is the post-modifier?
A the projected -- it comes before the noun.
B from the renegotiated freight contracts -- a prepositional phrase after the head noun "savings".
C savings -- it is the head noun, not a modifier.
3. Why do speakers use complex noun phrases in a proposal?
A To pack precise information densely and sound authoritative.
B To make the sentence longer for no reason.
C To avoid using any nouns at all.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Complex Noun Phrases

GRAMMAR

Como empacotar informação em um substantivo denso e preciso -- a marca de uma proposta com autoridade. / How to pack information into a dense, precise noun -- the hallmark of an authoritative proposal.

Parte / PartPosição / PositionExample / Exemplo
Pre-modifiers / pré-modificadoresantes do núcleo / before the head nouna fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal
Head noun / núcleoo substantivo central / the central nounthe projected savings / a phased rollout
Post-modifier (prep.) / pós (preposição)depois do núcleo / after the head nounthe savings from the renegotiated freight contracts
Post-modifier (particip.) / pó (particípio)depois do núcleo / after the head nouna phased rollout designed to minimize disruption
Regra / Key: clarezaEmpilhe no máximo 3-4 pré-modificadores -- mais que isso fica ambíguo. / Stack at most 3-4 pre-modifiers; more becomes ambiguous.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the right word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Lead with the number: "Let me start with the : four point two million in savings."
Give the reasons: "Here is the for moving from eleven suppliers to four."
Time to recover the cost: "The is under eleven months."
Support every claim: "Real figures every claim in the proposal."
Step by step: "I propose a , region by region."
Express as a number: "I have tried to every assumption in the model."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?To protect delivery, she proposes a staged approach -- a phased rollout region by region.
?Fabiana opens with the headline figure: four point two million in projected savings.
?She closes by saying the consolidation logic underpins every claim, so the case is compelling.
?She lays out the cost-benefit: a payback period under eleven months and a strong ROI.
?She gives the rationale: moving from eleven overlapping suppliers down to four partners.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me start with the headline figure: the projected savings come to four million dollars.
Deixe-me começar pelo número principal: a economia projetada chega a quatro milhões de dólares.
This is a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal.
Esta é uma proposta de consolidação de fornecedores de três anos, totalmente orçada.
I propose a phased rollout designed to minimize disruption, region by region.
Proponho uma implementação em fases concebida para minimizar a interrupção, região por região.
The cost-benefit is clear, and real figures underpin every claim in the business case.
O custo-benefício é claro, e números reais sustentam cada afirmação no caso de negócio.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You are opening a proposal to leadership and want to lead with the single most important number. You say:
A "Let me start with the headline figure: projected savings of four point two million over three years." (dense, leads with the number)
B "So, um, there are a few numbers and some of them are quite big." (vague, weak)
C "I will tell you the savings later if we have time." (buries the key number)
You want to pack the whole plan into one authoritative noun phrase. The best version is:
A "We have a plan, and it is about suppliers, and it lasts three years." (loose, not dense)
B "a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal" (pre-modifiers stacked before the head noun)
C "a proposal fully and three and costed and supplier the consolidation" (ambiguous, ungrammatical)
A board member asks why the proposal is low-risk. You want to stress the step-by-step plan. You say:
A "We use a staged approach -- a phased rollout designed to minimize disruption, region by region." (post-modifier after the head noun)
B "We just switch everything at once and hope for the best." (high-risk, weak)
C "I have not really thought about the risk yet." (no business case)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Pitch a strategic proposal from your work. Open with the headline figure, give the rationale, lay out the cost-benefit (payback period and return on investment), and close with a staged approach. Use at least three complex noun phrases (pre-modifiers + a "from / designed to" post-modifier).

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1Let me start with the headline figure: projected savings of four point two million.Deixe-me começar pelo número principal: economia projetada de quatro vírgula dois milhões.
2This is a fully costed three-year supplier-consolidation proposal.Esta é uma proposta de consolidação de fornecedores de três anos, totalmente orçada.
3The payback period is under eleven months, with a strong return on investment.O período de retorno é inferior a onze meses, com um forte retorno sobre o investimento.
4I propose a phased rollout designed to minimize disruption, region by region.Proponho uma implementação em fases concebida para minimizar a interrupção, região por região.
5Real figures underpin every claim, so the business case is compelling.Números reais sustentam cada afirmação, então o caso de negócio é convincente.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Negotiating Contracts and Terms -- Conditional Variations (Should the supplier miss the deadline, the penalty clause applies)

Bargaining conditionals for precise contract negotiation: inverted forms (Should + base verb for a real condition; Were + subject + to + base for a hypothetical offer) and condition connectors (provided that / as long as / on condition that / unless) -- trading a concession for a condition with professional pushback. New vocabulary: indemnity, bargaining chip, redline, liability cap, default, binding, terminate, waiver, force majeure, amendment, in good faith, penalty clause.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Indemnity -- a promise to cover the other party's losses or damages
"The supplier must give an indemnity against any third-party claim." (Indenização / garantia de ressarcimento)
Bargaining chip -- something you can offer or trade to gain an advantage in a deal
"The faster payment terms are our main bargaining chip." (Moeda de troca / trunfo de negociação)
Redline -- a marked change or objection to the wording of a draft contract
"We sent back the draft with a redline on the penalty wording." (Marcação de objeção / alteração no rascunho)
Liability cap -- a limit on how much one party must pay for damages
"Unless we agree a liability cap of two million per incident, I cannot sign." (Limite de responsabilidade)
Default -- a failure to fulfill a contractual obligation, such as a deadline
"Missing a confirmed deadline counts as a default, and the penalty applies." (Inadimplência / descumprimento)
Binding -- legally enforceable; you must follow it
"The concession must be binding, not just a verbal understanding." (Vinculante / obrigatório)
Terminate -- to end a contract before its natural close
"Either party may terminate on ninety days notice." (Rescindir / encerrar)
Waiver -- a formal voluntary giving up of a right
"The penalty applies in full, with no waiver." (Renúncia / dispensa)
Force majeure -- unforeseeable events that excuse a party from a duty
"The force majeure clause stays standard." (Força maior / caso fortuito)
Amendment -- a formal change made to an existing contract
"I will send the amendment for signature today." (Aditivo / emenda contratual)
In good faith -- honestly and fairly, with sincere intentions
"If they accept those points in good faith, we have a deal." (De boa-fé)
Penalty clause -- a fixed charge for failing to meet a contract term
"Should the supplier miss a deadline, the penalty clause applies." (Cláusula de multa / penalidade)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Indemnity
Bargaining chip
Redline
Liability cap
Default
Binding
Terminate
Waiver
Force majeure
Amendment
In good faith
Penalty clause

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

In a contract negotiation, the plain word if often sounds too casual. Formal English offers sharper, more powerful variations. To set a real condition, you can invert with Should: "Should the supplier miss a deadline, the penalty clause applies." Here Should + the supplier + miss (base verb) replaces "If the supplier misses". To make a hypothetical offer, you invert with Were: "Were you to bring the lead time down to ten days, we would halve the penalty." This means "If you brought it down". To attach a strict condition, use provided that, as long as, or on condition that: "We hold the rate provided that you commit to the volume." And unless means "if not": "Unless we agree a liability cap, I cannot sign." Notice: the inverted forms already contain the "if", so you never write "if were you to"; and "unless" already means "if not", so you never write "unless if". When the condition comes first, a comma separates it from the result.

1. "Should the supplier miss a deadline, the penalty applies." This condition is:
A real and formal -- an inverted way of saying "If the supplier misses a deadline".
B a pure hypothesis that will never happen.
C a command telling the supplier what to do.
2. Which sentence is correct?
A "Unless if we get a cap, we cannot sign."
B "Unless we get a cap, we cannot sign." -- "unless" already means "if not".
C "If unless we get a cap, we cannot sign."
3. Why use "Were you to bring the lead time down..." instead of "If you brought..."?
A It is a more formal, hypothetical way to make a conditional offer in a negotiation.
B It means the opposite of "If you brought it down".
C It is only used to talk about the past.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Conditional Variations

GRAMMAR

Como trocar uma concessão por uma condição com precisão formal -- a marca de uma negociação de contrato. / How to trade a concession for a condition with formal precision -- the hallmark of a contract negotiation.

Forma / FormSentido / MeaningExample / Exemplo
Should + sujeito + verbo basese acontecer (real, formal) / if it happensShould the supplier miss a deadline, the penalty applies.
Were + sujeito + to + basese você fizesse (hipotético) / if you didWere you to bring the lead time down, we would halve the penalty.
provided that / as long as / on condition thatsomente se isto for verdade / only if this is trueWe hold the rate provided that you commit to the volume.
unlessse não / a menos que (= if not)Unless we agree a cap, I cannot sign.
Regra / KeyAs formas invertidas já contêm o "if" -- nunca escreva "if were you to" nem "unless if". / The inverted forms already contain "if"; never write "if were you to" or "unless if".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the right word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Real condition: " the supplier miss a deadline, the penalty clause applies."
Hypothetical offer: " you to bring the lead time down to ten days, we would halve the penalty."
Strict condition: "We hold the rate you commit to the volume."
If not: " we agree a liability cap, I cannot recommend this."
Something to trade: "The faster payment terms are our main ."
End the contract: "Either party may on ninety days notice."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?As her bargaining chip, she offers to halve the penalty were the supplier to reach a ten-day lead time.
?Fabiana opens the contract review and sets out her position on price and terms.
?She insists on a liability cap and closes by summarizing the four-point counter-offer.
?She makes the penalty clause non-negotiable: should the supplier miss a deadline, the penalty applies.
?She offers to hold the rate, provided that the supplier commits to the renegotiated freight volume.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Should the supplier miss a confirmed deadline, the penalty clause applies.
Caso o fornecedor perca um prazo confirmado, a cláusula de multa se aplica.
Were you to bring the lead time down to ten days, we would halve the penalty.
Se você reduzisse o prazo de entrega para dez dias, nós reduziríamos a multa pela metade.
We can hold the rate, provided that you commit to the renegotiated volume.
Podemos manter a tarifa, desde que você se comprometa com o volume renegociado.
Unless we agree a liability cap, I cannot recommend this to the board.
A menos que concordemos com um limite de responsabilidade, não posso recomendar isto ao conselho.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You want to attach a penalty to a missed deadline in a formal, precise way. You say:
A "Should the supplier miss a confirmed deadline, the penalty clause applies, with no waiver." (inverted real condition)
B "If they are late, maybe we charge them something, I am not sure." (vague, weak)
C "We will see what happens if they miss it." (no binding term)
You want to offer a conditional concession in exchange for a faster lead time. The best version is:
A "If were you to cut the lead time, we would halve the penalty." (incorrect -- double "if")
B "Were you to bring the lead time down to ten days, we would halve the penalty." (inverted hypothetical)
C "We halve the penalty and you do whatever you want with the lead time." (no condition attached)
A supplier asks you to drop the liability cap. You want to hold firm. You say:
A "Unless we agree a liability cap, I cannot recommend this to the board." (firm condition, "if not")
B "Unless if we get a cap, no deal." (incorrect -- "unless if")
C "Fine, we can drop the cap, no problem." (gives away a key protection)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Make a four-point counter-offer for a real or imagined contract at your work. Hold one term "provided that" a condition is met, attach a penalty with "Should...", offer a concession with "Were you to...", and hold firm on a cap with "Unless...". Use at least three bargaining conditionals.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1We can hold the rate, provided that you commit to the renegotiated volume.Podemos manter a tarifa, desde que você se comprometa com o volume renegociado.
2Should the supplier miss a deadline, the penalty clause applies, with no waiver.Caso o fornecedor perca um prazo, a cláusula de multa se aplica, sem renúncia.
3Were you to bring the lead time down to ten days, we would halve the penalty.Se você reduzisse o prazo de entrega para dez dias, reduziríamos a multa pela metade.
4Unless we agree a liability cap, I cannot recommend this to the board.A menos que concordemos com um limite de responsabilidade, não posso recomendar isto ao conselho.
5Either party may terminate on condition that all deliverables are settled first.Qualquer das partes pode rescindir, com a condição de que todas as entregas sejam quitadas primeiro.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

End-to-End Communication -- Advanced Discourse Markers (signposting a coherent, extended turn)

Advanced discourse markers for telling the whole story coherently: sequence (to begin with / moving on), contrast (that said / nonetheless), result (consequently / as a result), and summary (to sum up / the upshot is) -- the four moves that hold an extended turn together. New vocabulary: discourse marker, cohesion, signpost, throughline, synthesize, recap, segue, crux, overarching, upshot, standpoint, coherent.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Discourse marker -- a word or phrase that signals the next stage of speech
"Use a discourse marker like 'moving on' to start a new stage." (Marcador discursivo / conector)
Cohesion -- the quality of parts hanging together as a whole
"The cohesion of the talk made it easy to follow." (Coesão)
Signpost -- to mark out the stages so listeners can follow
"Let me signpost where we are going: first the context, then the proposal." (Sinalizar / orientar)
Throughline -- the single idea that runs through the whole story
"The throughline is simple: every decision was underpinned by real figures." (Fio condutor)
Synthesize -- to combine separate parts into one clear whole
"In the recap, I synthesize the whole programme in two sentences." (Sintetizar)
Recap -- a short summary of what was already said
"To finish, a quick recap of the headline figures." (Recapitulação / resumo)
Segue -- a smooth transition from one topic to the next
"That is a natural segue from the negotiation to the results." (Transição suave / gancho)
Crux -- the single most important point of the matter
"The crux of the problem was duplication across eleven suppliers." (Âmago / ponto central)
Overarching -- covering and connecting everything; top-level
"The overarching aim was simple: fewer partners, better rates." (Abrangente / global)
Upshot -- the final result or conclusion that matters
"The upshot is that both sides signed in good faith." (Resultado final / conclusão)
Standpoint -- a particular point of view on a matter
"From a cost standpoint, the consolidation is clearly the right call." (Ponto de vista / ótica)
Coherent -- clear, logical, and easy to follow as a whole
"A coherent story takes them from the problem to the signature without gaps." (Coerente)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Discourse marker
Cohesion
Signpost
Throughline
Synthesize
Recap
Segue
Crux
Overarching
Upshot
Standpoint
Coherent

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

An extended turn -- presenting a whole programme from problem to result -- only works if it is coherent. The tool that creates that cohesion is the discourse marker: a word or phrase that signals what comes next. There are four key functions. To signpost a new stage, use a sequence marker: "To begin with, the context. Moving on, the proposal." To turn the direction, use a contrast marker: "The savings were clear. That said, the negotiation was tough." (Others: nonetheless, whereas, having said that.) To show an effect, use a result marker: "We held firm on the cap. Consequently, the contract is balanced." (Others: as a result, in light of this.) And to close, use a summary marker: "To sum up, four million in savings." (Others: in essence, the upshot is.) Match the marker to the function -- a contrast marker where you mean a result simply confuses the listener. Used well, these markers give your story a clear throughline from the first word to the final recap.

1. "We held firm on the cap. Consequently, the contract is balanced." What does "Consequently" signal?
A a result -- the balanced contract follows as an effect of holding firm.
B a contrast -- the two ideas push against each other.
C a new, unrelated topic.
2. Which marker best opens a new stage of the story (sequence)?
A "That said," -- this signals a contrast.
B "Moving on," -- this signals the next stage.
C "As a result," -- this signals an effect.
3. Why do speakers use discourse markers in an extended turn?
A To create cohesion and a clear throughline so listeners can follow from start to finish.
B To make the talk longer with no purpose.
C To hide the main point from the audience.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Advanced Discourse Markers

GRAMMAR

Como organizar um turno longo em quatro funções -- a marca de uma comunicação coerente de ponta a ponta. / How to organize a long turn into four functions -- the hallmark of coherent, end-to-end communication.

Função / FunctionMarkersExample / Exemplo
Sequence / sequência (novo estágio)to begin with / moving on / turning toMoving on, the proposal.
Contrast / contrastethat said / nonetheless / whereasThat said, the negotiation was tough.
Result / resultadoconsequently / as a result / in light of thisConsequently, the contract is balanced.
Summary / resumoto sum up / in essence / the upshot isTo sum up, four million in savings.
Regra / KeyCombine o marcador com a função e use vírgula após o marcador inicial. / Match the marker to the function; put a comma after the opening marker.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the right word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Mark out the stages: "Let me where we are going: first the context, then the proposal."
The connecting idea: "The is simple: every decision was underpinned by real figures."
Combine into one: "In the recap, I the whole programme in two sentences."
The result that matters: "The is that both sides signed in good faith."
The central point: "The of the problem was duplication across eleven suppliers."
A smooth transition: "That is a natural from the negotiation to the results."

Stage 2: Put the Story in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the events in the correct order.

?As a result, both sides signed, and she recaps the headline figures to sum up.
?To begin with, Fabiana signposts the context: eleven overlapping suppliers and the crux of the problem.
?Then she covers the negotiation, trading a bargaining chip for a faster lead time.
?Moving on, she presents the proposal: a fully costed three-year consolidation plan.
?She ties the whole throughline together, from standpoint to signature.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me signpost where we are going: to begin with, the context.
Deixe-me orientar para onde vamos: para começar, o contexto.
That said, the negotiation was tough; as a result, both sides signed.
Dito isso, a negociação foi difícil; como resultado, ambos os lados assinaram.
The throughline is simple: every decision was underpinned by real figures.
O fio condutor é simples: cada decisão foi sustentada por números reais.
To sum up: from eleven suppliers to four, four million in savings, contract signed.
Resumindo: de onze fornecedores para quatro, quatro milhões em economia, contrato assinado.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each professional situation.

You are opening the first stage of an end-to-end presentation. You say:
A "Let me signpost where we are going. To begin with, the context: eleven overlapping suppliers." (clear sequence marker)
B "So, there is a lot of stuff and I will just say it all and see how it goes." (no signposting)
C "As a result, the context is eleven suppliers." (result marker used for an opening -- wrong function)
You want to connect a tough negotiation to a successful outcome. The best version is:
A "The negotiation was tough. That said, both sides signed." (contrast marker used for a result)
B "The negotiation was tough. As a result, both sides signed in good faith." (correct result marker)
C "The negotiation was tough both sides signed." (no marker, no comma -- unclear)
You are closing the whole story with a sharp recap. You say:
A "To sum up: from eleven suppliers to four, four million in savings, contract signed." (summary marker + synthesis)
B "Moving on, from eleven suppliers to four." (sequence marker used to close -- wrong function)
C "And there were some numbers and it went fine, I think." (no synthesis, no figures)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Present a real or imagined project from your work end to end in about 90 seconds. Open each stage with a sequence marker (to begin with / moving on), use a contrast marker for the hard part (that said), a result marker for the outcome (as a result), and close with a recap (to sum up) that synthesizes the headline figures. Aim for a clear throughline.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20
