1Let me signpost where we are going: to begin with, the context.Deixe-me orientar para onde vamos: para começar, o contexto.
2Moving on to the proposal, the overarching aim was fewer partners, better rates.Passando para a proposta, o objetivo central era menos parceiros, melhores tarifas.
3That said, the negotiation was tough; as a result, both sides signed in good faith.Dito isso, a negociação foi difícil; como resultado, ambos assinaram de boa-fé.
4The throughline is simple: every decision was underpinned by real figures.O fio condutor é simples: cada decisão foi sustentada por números reais.
5To sum up: from eleven suppliers to four, four million in savings, contract signed.Resumindo: de onze fornecedores para quatro, quatro milhões em economia, contrato assinado.

Atividades Complementares

Filmes, séries e podcasts para reforçar cada aula

Lesson 1 — Diagnostic + Who Is Fabiana? -- Mapping Your English

Series
Industry -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Sesh"

Young graduates navigating the corporate world in a global finance company. The high-pressure environment mirrors Bloomberg's pace, and the financial vocabulary is directly applicable to your context.

Watch with English subtitles. Pay attention to how characters introduce themselves and describe their roles in the first 15 minutes.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Supply Chain Now — Different Ways of Finding Supply Chain (Rutgers University)

Three Rutgers professionals — Joseph Agresta, Nikki Gaboff, and Haley Carlsen — describe how they got into supply chain and what the work really involves: project management, demand forecasting, risk management, and building peer relationships across industries. Great for hearing real professionals describe their day-to-day roles.

Note how each speaker describes their daily tasks — jot down 5 action verbs they use (forecast, source, coordinate, manage, plan).

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
How Does The Bloomberg Terminal Work?

A 10-minute explainer about the Bloomberg Terminal, the core product your company provides. Hearing your own company's product described in English builds confidence and activates passive vocabulary you already know.

Watch once for fun, then watch again and pause every time you hear a word from today's lesson (supply chain, vendor, procurement, etc.).

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — First Contact -- Introducing Yourself to International Suppliers

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 2: "Errors and Omissions"

Harvey and Mike navigate their first client meeting together. Watch how professionals introduce themselves, establish credibility, and handle unexpected questions. The legal vocabulary parallels supply chain contract language.

Focus on how Harvey frames his introductions differently depending on who he is talking to. Notice the third conditional in retrospective discussions: "If we had filed earlier..."

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Supply Chain Now — Raising the Bar on Value: How Sourcing Can Respond in Dynamic Markets

Ahmad Jiwani of Coupa joins hosts Scott Luton and Greg White to discuss how strategic sourcing programs evaluate complex supplier bids on cost, delivery times, and diversity, and build agile, resilient supply chains in volatile markets. Listen for sourcing and procurement vocabulary in real business context.

Listen for terms like strategic sourcing, supplier bid, lead time, and resilience — pause and repeat each one aloud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
How to Make the Best First Impressions

A 7-minute video on the psychology of first impressions in professional settings. Directly applicable to vendor introductions and initial meetings with international partners.

After watching, practice introducing yourself using the techniques mentioned. Record a 60-second self-introduction as if meeting Klaus from TechFlow for the first time.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — When Things Go Wrong -- Handling Delays and Escalations

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 3, Episode 23: "Beach Games"

Watch how managers handle an unexpected crisis and shifting accountability. Notice how characters reflect on what they "should have" or "could have" done differently when a plan falls apart.

Write down every modal perfect you hear ("should have", "could have", "must have"). Pause and repeat each one aloud, then create your own version about a supply chain delay.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Supply Chain Now — 6 Strategies for Greater Supply Chain Resilience, Part 1

TEKTOK host Karin Bursa presents the first three of six strategies for building resilience: sensing and responding to market changes, optimizing inventory and capacity network-wide, and multi-sourcing to reduce single-supplier dependency. Directly on the card's disruption-and-contingency theme.

Listen for the three strategies and the vocabulary around them (buffer, multi-sourcing, nearshoring); summarize each in your own words.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
How to Deliver Bad News (Project Management Training)

A short video on communicating clearly under pressure, escalating issues, and taking accountability. Directly applicable to managing supplier delays and explaining setbacks to senior management.

After watching, record a 60-second escalation message about a delayed shipment. Use at least three new words and two modal perfects.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Navigating Hierarchy -- Communicating Across Levels

Series
Succession -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Celebration"

A masterclass in corporate hierarchy: who reports to whom, who holds the real mandate, and how people navigate power across levels. Notice how characters align, build rapport, and seek buy-in before big decisions.

Watch with English subtitles. Note every time someone goes around the chain of command -- and what happens next. Then describe one scene using three new words from today.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — The Conversations You Should Be Having with Your Manager

Executive coach Melody Wilding, author of 'Managing Up,' explains how to communicate with your boss and skip-level managers — finding alignment, gaining visibility, and building buy-in.

Listen for how she frames requests and positions herself as a trusted partner to win buy-in.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
How to Work with Someone You Can't Stand (HBR Guide)

A short, practical video on reading an organization, finding the right person with the mandate, and influencing across levels. Directly applicable to working with international suppliers like TechFlow.

After watching, record a 60-second briefing about a supplier's hierarchy. Use at least three new words and two WISH structures ("I wish I knew..." / "I wish I had...").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — The Vendor Call -- Running International Supply Chain Meetings

Series
Industry -- Season 2, Episode 3: "Wargames"

Tense client and vendor calls where prices are renegotiated and terms are pushed under pressure. Listen for how professionals run a structured call, set an agenda, and close with clear next steps.

Watch with English subtitles. Note every passive sentence ("the deal was closed", "the terms were changed") and one moment where someone has work done by another team. Then describe one scene using three new words from today.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Supply Chain Now — Collaborating with Suppliers Through Challenging Times

A Georgia-Pacific procurement executive, a contracts expert, and an industry analyst discuss how to build supplier relationships, structure contracts, and run post-issue reviews to prevent problems from recurring. Practical for hearing how professionals handle supplier conversations and negotiations.

Focus on phrases used to open and close supplier discussions and to document action items — repeat them for a mock supplier call.

Watch on YouTube ↗

YouTube
How to Run an Effective Meeting: 5 Tips

A short, practical video on chairing a meeting: setting the agenda, keeping the discussion on track, and ending with owned action items. Directly applicable to running calls with international suppliers like TechFlow.

After watching, record a 60-second mock vendor call closing. Use at least three new words, two passive sentences, and one causative ("we had ... done" / "I will get ... done").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Making Your Case -- Persuasive Language in Negotiations

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 4: "Dirty Little Secrets"

High-stakes negotiations where lawyers make their case, propose trade-offs, and hold a bottom line. Listen for how they emphasize one point at a time and steer a deadlock toward a deal.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two cleft sentences you hear ("It is ... that ...", "What I need is ..."). Then describe one negotiation scene using three new words from today.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Negotiate Anything — How to Build a Strategic Narrative for Negotiations with Lisa Gates

Leadership storyteller Lisa Gates shows how framing and a strategic narrative make your case — stating your value proposition and turning a deadlock into collaboration.

Notice how she frames a request as a story rather than a flat demand.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED Talk -- "How to Speak So That People Want to Listen" (Julian Treasure)

A 10-minute talk on vocal techniques and persuasive speaking. Directly applicable to vendor negotiations and board presentations where emphasis and clarity determine outcomes.

After watching, practice your negotiation pitch from today's role-play using the vocal techniques. Record a 90-second version.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 7 — Saying the Unsaid -- Emphasis and Focus in Meetings

Series
Succession -- Season 2, Episode 5: "Tern Haven"

A cross-functional, high-stakes meeting where almost nothing important is said directly. The real message lives in the subtext, the pushback, and the elephant in the room nobody names. A masterclass in reading the unsaid.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down two moments where a character downplays or sugarcoats something. Then rewrite one line as a direct sentence, and one as an emphatic inversion ("Never have I...", "Not only... but also...").

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — Is it Safe to Speak Up at Work?

Adam Grant and Amy Edmondson explore how to speak up and give candid feedback without blame, and how leaders build the psychological safety that makes honest disagreement possible.

Listen for how people raise concerns tactfully — softeners used before candid feedback.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED Talk -- "The Power of Vulnerability" (Brene Brown)

A 20-minute talk on how vulnerability and transparency build stronger relationships. Brene Brown uses emphatic language to drive her points home. Applicable to how you communicate difficult truths in professional settings.

Note how Brene Brown uses sentence structure for emphasis. After watching, practice opening a meeting about a failure using at least 3 inverted sentences.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 8 — What Should Have Happened -- Reviewing Supply Chain Failures

Series
The Playlist -- Episode 4: "The Coder"

A real product post-mortem told from one engineer's point of view: a system broke, a launch slipped, and the team replays what should have happened. A clear look at root cause, fallout, and corrective action.

Watch with English subtitles. Note two moments where someone explains a past mistake. Then rewrite each one as a mixed conditional: one with "If we had..., we would not be..." and one with "If ... were ..., ... would have...".

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Freakonomics Radio — What Happens When Everyone Stays Home to Eat?

When the pandemic forced everyone to eat at home, a sudden demand shift broke parts of the U.S. food supply chain. The episode traces the winners, the losers, and the ripple effects of that shock through the system.

Listen for cause-and-effect language describing how one disruption spreads through an entire system.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
How to Run a Project Post-Mortem Meeting

A short, practical video on reviewing a failure without blame: finding the root cause, documenting lessons learned, and setting corrective action. A template you can reuse for your own post-mortems.

After watching, record a 60-second post-mortem of a fictional disruption. Use at least three new words and three mixed conditionals -- two of the type "If we had..." and one of the type "If ... were ... would have...".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Habits, Changes, and Adaptations -- Processes That Evolved

Series
The Office (US) -- "Email Surveillance" (Season 3)

A company tries to phase out an old habit and roll out a new workflow, and the team struggles to get used to it. A light, clear example of how a transition meets resistance before people adapt.

Watch with English subtitles. Note one thing the office used to do and one new way they have to get used to. Then say each one aloud: one sentence with "used to", one with "get used to + -ing".

Disponível no streaming: Apple TV+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — The Leadership Skills That Make Transformation Stick

BCG's Julia Dhar explains why organizational change so often fails — the problem is usually behavior, not strategy — and how leaders get people willing, motivated, and equipped to adopt a new way of working and sustain the momentum after the novelty fades.

As you listen, jot the three conditions Dhar says people need to adopt change, then map them to a change happening at your work.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
What is Fintech? | CNBC Explains

A short, practical video on how to automate routine steps, streamline approvals, and run a rollout in iterations so people can get used to the change. A template you can reuse for your own process work.

After watching, record a 60-second comparison of an old and a new process at your work. Use at least four new words and three structures: one "used to", one "would" for a repeated past action, and one "get used to + -ing".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Projecting Forward -- Timelines, Forecasts, and Strategic Plans

Series
Billions -- "The Future" (Season 5, planning episodes)

Executives present projections, defend a roadmap, and argue over the outlook for the next quarter. A sharp example of how leaders talk about timelines, targets, and scenarios under pressure.

Watch with English subtitles. Note one projection and one risk a character names. Then say each aloud: one sentence with the future perfect ("By Q4, we will have...") and one with the future continuous ("This time next year, we will be...").

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Future-Proofing Your Strategy with Scenario Planning

Peter Scoblic of Event Horizon Strategies shows how to build a strategy that holds up against different futures, using the U.S. Coast Guard as a case study of ongoing scenario planning and stress-testing plans against multiple plausible outcomes.

Pick one decision you're facing and, as Scoblic suggests, sketch two very different future scenarios to test it against.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED Talk -- "How Great Leaders Inspire Action" (Simon Sinek)

A classic 18-minute talk on visionary leadership. Sinek uses future-oriented language throughout to describe where organizations are headed. Directly applicable to presenting strategic plans.

After watching, prepare a 2-minute strategic plan presentation for a fictional Bloomberg initiative using all 6 future forms from today's lesson.

Watch on TED ↗

Lesson 11 — Reading the Room -- Relative Clauses in Cross-Cultural Negotiations

Series
Shogun (2024) -- Episode 1: "Anjin"

A masterclass in high-context, cross-cultural communication. Watch how meaning is carried by silence, etiquette, and indirect language, and how characters must read between the lines to survive negotiations.

Note three moments where a character says one thing but means another. How would you describe each person using a relative clause (the lord who..., the translator whose...)?

Disponível no streaming: Disney+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Cross-Culture Work in a Global Economy

INSEAD's Erin Meyer, author of 'The Culture Map,' explains how directness, context, and reciprocity differ across cultures and how to adapt when negotiating and working globally.

Listen for how she contrasts high-context and low-context cultures with concrete examples.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Erin Meyer: Working Across Cultures

The author of The Culture Map explains how communication, persuasion, and trust differ across cultures. Essential framing for anyone negotiating with international vendors.

After watching, prepare a 2-minute briefing for a colleague on the etiquette of one high-context culture, using at least four relative clauses.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — The Risk Conversation -- Modals of Probability and Deduction

Series
Chernobyl (2019) -- Episode 1: "1:23:45"

A masterclass in risk denial and crisis management. Watch how leaders weigh likelihood against impact, ignore a clear vulnerability, and get caught off guard when the worst case arrives.

Note three moments where someone underestimates a threat. Rewrite each as a modal of probability (it could..., it must..., it can't...).

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Creating More Resilient Supply Chains

Harvard's Willy Shih explains how global, just-in-time supply chains break under shocks and how companies can build buffers and hedge against disruption in an uncertain world.

Listen for risk vocabulary — 'buffer', 'disruption', 'resilience', 'hedge' — describing how firms manage shocks.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Risk, Resilience and AI in the Supply Chain (Yossi Sheffi, MIT)

An MIT session on identifying exposure, weighing likelihood vs impact, and designing safeguards and fallbacks for resilient supply chains. Essential framing for any sourcing decision.

After watching, prepare a 2-minute risk register for one of your own projects, using a different modal of probability in each line.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 13 — The Decision Meeting -- Reported Speech, Recommending and Reaching Consensus

Series
Succession (2018) -- Boardroom & vote-counting scenes

A masterclass in steering a room to a decision. Watch how characters relay what others said, count votes, recommend, abstain, and try to avoid deadlock under pressure.

Pick one scene and report three lines as reported speech (she said that..., he asked whether..., they recommended that...).

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — When Over-Collaboration Leads to Indecision

Leadership coach Rebecca Shambaugh explains how the drive for consensus can stall decisions, and how teams handle dissent and break decision deadlock without over-collaborating. Real professional English on debate versus agreement.

Note the phrases used to disagree politely and to push a group toward a decision.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
3 Simple Ways to Make Your Meetings and Decision-Making Better

A short HBR guide on structuring a decision meeting: framing the options, gathering input, reaching consensus, and recording who agreed to what. Essential framing for any sourcing or vendor decision.

After watching, write 5 lines of meeting minutes for one of your own decisions, using a different reported-speech structure in each line.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Negotiating the Contract -- Diplomatic Language: Softening and Hedging

Series
Suits (2011) -- Contract negotiation scenes

A masterclass in diplomatic pressure. Watch how the lawyers push back, soften demands, concede small points, and protect the relationship while still winning the terms they need.

Pick one negotiation scene and rewrite three blunt lines as diplomatic ones (might be a little..., I was wondering whether..., it might be worth...).

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Negotiate Anything — Exploring Conflict Resilience: A New Approach to Negotiating and Mediation with Bob Bordone

Negotiation experts Bob Bordone and Simon Dowling explain 'conflict resilience' — how to soften disagreements, stay empathetic, and keep the other side amenable while still making your point.

Notice how the guests reframe a hard 'no' into a softer, relationship-preserving response.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Dealing With Disagreements in Advanced Business English

A short guide to the exact phrases that soften disagreement, qualify a claim, and turn a demand into a polite request -- the core moves of diplomatic negotiation.

After watching, draft a short term sheet for a real negotiation, using a different softening move for price, penalty, and delivery.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Managing Conflict at Work -- Concession & Contrast Clauses

Series
The Office (US) -- Conflict-resolution episodes

A study in how NOT to manage conflict, and occasionally how to. Watch how characters trigger defensiveness, assign blame, and reach a standoff -- then how a calmer voice de-escalates and rebuilds goodwill.

Pick one tense scene and rewrite two blaming lines as concession sentences (Although..., Despite..., Whereas...).

Disponível no streaming: Prime Video

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — Is it Safe to Speak Up at Work?

Adam Grant and Amy Edmondson explore how to speak up and give candid feedback without blame, and how leaders build the psychological safety that makes honest disagreement possible.

Listen for how people raise concerns tactfully — softeners used before candid feedback.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
"Linking Words: Although, Even Though, Despite, In Spite Of" -- English Grammar

A clear guide to the exact difference that trips up B2 speakers: a clause after although / even though / while / whereas, but a noun or -ing after despite / in spite of.

After watching, write five concession sentences about a real work conflict, using a different linker in each.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Leading a Project Kick-off -- Future Continuous (will be + -ing)

Series
Halt and Catch Fire -- Project launch episodes

A drama about teams starting ambitious tech projects. Watch how leaders set direction at a kick-off, assign ownership, and struggle with dependencies and scope creep as the work ramps up.

Pick one launch scene and write three sentences in the future continuous describing what the team will be doing next.

Streaming no Brasil: verificar disponibilidade

Podcast
Projectified — How Project Managers Spot Red Flags and Get Ahead of Them

Two PMPs discuss how to spot early warning signs on a project — resource misallocation, scope creep and misalignment with strategy — and how to raise them with leadership before they escalate.

Note down each red flag they mention and how they suggest handling it.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Future Continuous and Future Perfect Explained

A clear guide to the exact contrast in this lesson: will be + verb-ing for an action in progress at a future point, versus will have + past participle for an action finished by then.

After watching, write a six-line kick-off opening for a real project, using will be + -ing in four lines and will have + participle in one.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Disruption Management -- The Subjunctive (It is essential that + base verb)

Series
The Playlist (Netflix) -- The crisis-management episodes

A drama about a company facing a sudden legal and reputational threat. Watch how leaders escalate, run containment, weigh the fallout, and decide who gets briefed first -- the same choices you practiced today.

Pick one crisis scene and write three sentences using the subjunctive: "It is essential that ...", "I recommend that ...", "We ask that ...".

Disponível no streaming: Netflix

Podcast
The Leading in a Crisis Podcast — EP85: Orange County Fire PIO Shares Stories From Mass Evacuation Incident, Part 1

A fire-department PIO shares first-hand stories from the opening hours of a mass-evacuation incident, when 50,000 residents had to leave and communication became an operational, life-safety function.

Listen for how the PIO decides what to communicate first under pressure.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
The Subjunctive in English (Advanced Grammar)

A clear guide to the exact structure in this lesson: the mandative subjunctive after essential, critical, recommend, and insist -- why we say "that he be" and "that she provide" with the base form and no -s.

After watching, write a six-line crisis email to leadership using the subjunctive in at least four lines.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Strategic Proposals -- Complex Noun Phrases (a phased rollout designed to minimize disruption)

Series
Billions (Showtime) -- The pitch-room episodes

Watch the scenes where analysts present an investment case to leadership: a headline figure first, then the rationale, the cost-benefit, and a staged approach. Notice how the strongest speakers pack precise information into dense noun phrases -- "a fully modeled three-year consolidation plan" -- exactly what you practiced today.

Pick one pitch and write three complex noun phrases from it (pre-modifiers + a post-modifying "from / designed to" phrase).

Disponível no streaming: Paramount+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Getting Buy-In for Your Next Big Idea

Experts share road-tested tactics for making the case for an idea: framing the issue, quantifying the value, tailoring the pitch to your audience, and involving the right people to win support. Exactly the language of building a business case.

Listen for how they open with the headline benefit before the details.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Noun Phrases in Academic Writing

A clear guide to the exact structure in this lesson: how to pack information before the head noun (pre-modifiers) and after it (post-modifying "of / from / designed to" phrases) to build the precise, authoritative noun phrases of a written proposal -- "the projected savings from the renegotiated freight contracts".

After watching, write a four-line proposal summary in which every line contains at least one complex noun phrase with both a pre-modifier and a post-modifier.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Negotiating Contracts and Terms -- Conditional Variations (Should the supplier miss the deadline, the penalty clause applies)

Series
Suits (USA Network) -- The negotiation and closing scenes

Watch the scenes where the lawyers negotiate terms: holding firm on a clause, trading a concession for a condition, and refusing to sign unless a cap is in place. Notice how the strongest negotiators tie every "yes" to an "if" -- "provided that", "should they breach", "unless we agree" -- exactly the bargaining conditionals you practiced today.

Pick one negotiation and write three conditional offers from it, one using "Should...", one using "Were... to...", and one using "provided that" or "unless".

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
Negotiate Anything — 1 Hour Straight of Negotiation Gold with Kwame Christian and Codie Sanchez

An hour of deal-making tactics with Kwame Christian and entrepreneur Codie Sanchez: trading concessions strategically, holding firm on the points that matter, and closing in good faith.

Track each moment someone gives a concession — what condition do they attach to it?

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
English Grammar - Inversion (Had I known..., Should you need...)

A clear guide to the exact structures in this lesson: how "Should the supplier miss..." and "Were you to bring..." replace plain "if" in formal English, plus "provided that", "as long as", "on condition that" and "unless" -- the precise conditional register of a contract negotiation.

After watching, write a four-line term sheet in which every line contains a different bargaining conditional (Should / Were / provided that / unless).

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — End-to-End Communication -- Advanced Discourse Markers (signposting a coherent, extended turn)

Series
The Newsroom (HBO) -- The end-of-episode wrap-ups

Watch how the anchors take a complex story from the opening problem to the final point without a single gap, signposting each stage and closing with a sharp recap. Notice the discourse markers that give the whole piece its cohesion and throughline -- exactly what you practiced today.

Pick one segment and write the four markers used: a sequence one, a contrast one, a result one, and a summary one.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
TED Talks Daily — How I climbed a 3,000-foot vertical cliff, without ropes | Alex Honnold

Free-solo climber Alex Honnold walks through one continuous story: how he prepared for and completed his ropeless ascent of Yosemite's El Capitan, from first idea to summit. One narrative told from beginning to end.

Note the signpost phrases he uses to move between stages ('first', 'then', 'the hardest part was') and how he wraps up with the payoff.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Discourse Markers: Sound Fluent & Natural in English

A clear guide to the exact structures in this lesson: the four functions of discourse markers (sequence, contrast, result, summary) and how they create the cohesion of a coherent, extended turn -- to begin with, that said, consequently, to sum up.

After watching, record a 90-second end-to-end summary of a project, using at least one marker of each of the four functions.

Assistir no YouTube ↗
